section_summaries.heading: Topics

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: What is AC
section_summaries.summary: Quote from Ross Anderson (text “Security Engineering”) Its function is to control which principals (persons, processes, machines, …) have access to which resources in the system -- which files they can read, which programs they can execute, and how they share data with other...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: Access Control is Pervasive
section_summaries.summary: - Application - business applications - Middleware - DBMS - Operating System - controlling access to files, ports - Hardware - memory protection, privilege levels

section_summaries.heading: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology
section_summaries.summary: Access Control Matrix – A general model for protection systems Lampson’1971 “Protection” Refined by Graham and Denning’1972 “Protection---Principles and Practice” Harrison, Ruzzo, and Ullman’1976 “Protection in Operating Systems”

section_summaries.heading: Overview
section_summaries.summary: - Protection state of system - Describes current settings, values of system relevant to protection - Access control matrix - Describes protection state precisely - Matrix describing rights of subjects - State transitions change elements of matrix

section_summaries.heading: Access Matrix
section_summaries.summary: - A set of subjects S - A set of objects O - A set of rights R - An access control matrix - one row for each subject - one column for each subject/object - elements are right of subject on another subject or object

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: Description
section_summaries.summary: - Subjects S = { s1,…,sn } - Objects O = { o1,…,om } - Rights R = { r1,…,rk } - Entries A[si, oj] ⊆ R - A[si, oj] = { rx, …, ry } means subject si has rights rx, …, ry over object oj

section_summaries.heading: Example 1
section_summaries.summary: - Processes p, q - Files f, g - Rights r, w, x, a, o | | f | g | p | q | |---|---|---|---|---| | p | rwo | r | rwxo | w | | q | a | ro | r | rwxo |

section_summaries.heading: Example 2
section_summaries.summary: Procedures inc_ctr, dec_ctr, manage Variable counter Rights +, –, call | | counter | inc_ctr | dec_ctr | manage | |------------|---------|---------|---------|--------| | inc_ctr | + | | | | | dec_ctr | – | | | | | manage | | call | call | call |

section_summaries.heading: Implementation
section_summaries.summary: Storing the access matrix - by rows: capability lists - by column: access control lists - through indirection: - e.g., key and lock list - e.g., groups, roles, multiple level of indirections, multiple locks How to do indirection correctly and conveniently is the key to...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: Implementation
section_summaries.summary: Access Control List (column) | File 1 | File 2 | |---|---| | Joe:Read | Joe:Read | | Joe:Write | Sam:Read | | Joe:Own | | | | Sam:Write | | | Sam:Own | Capability List (row) - Joe: File 1/Read, File 1/Write, File 1/Own, File 2/Read - Sam: File 2/Read, File 2/Write, File 2/Own...

section_summaries.heading: Access control lists
section_summaries.summary: - ACL is a list of permissions attached to an object - Who can modify the object's ACL? - What changes are allowed?

section_summaries.heading: Owners and Groups
section_summaries.summary: Who can modify the object's ACL? - One way is by introducing owners of objects - With ACLs we can define any combination of access, but that makes them difficult to manage - Group allow relatively fine-grained access control while making ACLs easier to manage - Owners and...

section_summaries.heading: Capability lists
section_summaries.summary: One way to partition the matrix is by rows. All access rights of one user together, stored in a data structure called a capability list Lists all the access rights or capabilities that a user has.

section_summaries.heading: Capability lists
section_summaries.summary: Sep 2009 Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - All access to objects is done through capabilities - Every program holds a set of capabilities - Each program holds a small number of capabilities - The only way a program can obtain capabilities is...

section_summaries.heading: Harrison-Ruzzo-Ullman model
section_summaries.summary: Discretionary Access Control Rights defined on specific (subject, object), decided by individual owners (as oppose to Mandatory Access Control, decided by system policies) HRU work - Formulating access matrices, towards Operating Systems - Provide a model that is sufficiently...

section_summaries.heading: Primitive Operations
section_summaries.summary: - create subject s; create object o - Creates new row, column in ACM; creates new column in ACM - destroy subject s; destroy object o - Deletes row, column from ACM; deletes column from ACM - enter r into A[s, o] - Adds r rights for subject s over object o - delete r from A[s,...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: Creating File
section_summaries.summary: - Process p creates file f with r and w permission - `command create•file(p, f)` - create object f; - enter own into A[p, f]; - enter r into A[p, f]; - enter w into A[p, f]; - end

section_summaries.heading: Mono-Operational Commands
section_summaries.summary: Make process p the owner of file g - `command make•owner(p, g)` - `enter own into A[p, g];` - end Mono-operational command Single primitive operation in this command

section_summaries.heading: Conditional Commands
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - Let p give q r rights over f, if p owns f - command grant•read•file•1(p, f, q) - if own in A[p, f] - then - enter r into A[q, f]; - end - Mono-conditional command - Single condition in this command

section_summaries.heading: Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
section_summaries.summary: - No precise definition - Widely used in modern operating systems - Often has the notion of owner of an object - The owner controls other users’ accesses to the object - Allows access rights to be propagated to other subjects

section_summaries.heading: Drawbacks in DAC
section_summaries.summary: - DAC cannot protect against - Trojan horse - Malware - Software bugs - Malicious local users - Cannot control information flow

section_summaries.heading: Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
section_summaries.summary: Sep 2009 Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: Mandatory Access Control
section_summaries.summary: Objects: security classification - e.g., grades=(confidential, {student-info}) Subjects: security clearances - e.g., Joe=(confidential, {student-info}) Access rules: defined by comparing the security classification of the requested objects with the security clearance of the...

section_summaries.heading: Mandatory Access Control
section_summaries.summary: - If access control rules are satisfied, access is permitted - e.g., Joe wants to read grades. - label(Joe)=(confidential,{student-info}) - label(grades)=(confidential,{student-info}) - Joe is permitted to read grades Granularity of access rights!

section_summaries.heading: Mandatory Access Control
section_summaries.summary: - Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - Security Classes (labels): (A,C) - A – total order authority level - C – set of categories - e.g., A = confidential > public , C = {student-info, dept-info} - (confidential,{ }) - (confidential,{dept-info})...

section_summaries.heading: Mandatory Access Control
section_summaries.summary: Dominance (≥): label l=(A,C) dominates l’=(A’,C’) iff A ≥ A’ and C ⊇ C’ - e.g., (confidential,{student-info}) ≥ (public,{student-info}) - BUT NOT (confidential, {student-info}) ≥ (public,{student-info, department-info})

section_summaries.heading: Bell- LaPadula (BLP) Model
section_summaries.summary: - Confidentiality protection - Lattice-based access control - Subjects - Objects - Security labels - Supports decentralized administration

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: BLP Reference Monitor
section_summaries.summary: - All accesses are controlled by the reference monitor - Cannot be bypassed - Access is allowed iff the resulting system state satisfies all security properties - Trusted subjects: subjects trusted not to compromise security

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: BLP Axioms 1.
section_summaries.summary: - Simple-security property: a subject s is allowed to read an object o only if the security label of s dominates the security label of o - No read up - Applies to all subjects - Subject s can read object o iff L(o) ≤ L(s) and s has permission to read o - Note: combines...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - *-property: a subject s is allowed to write an object o only if the security label of o dominates the security label of s - No write down - Applies to un-trusted subjects only - Subject s can write object o...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: Example
section_summaries.summary: - Tamara can read all files - Claire cannot read Personnel or E-Mail Files - Ulaley can only read Telephone Lists

section_summaries.heading: Levels and Lattices
section_summaries.summary: - Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - Security level is (clearance, category set) - ( Top Secret, { NUC, EUR, ASI } ) - ( Confidential, { EUR, ASI } ) - ( Secret, { NUC, ASI } ) - (A, C) dom (A′, C′) iff A′ ≤ A and C′ ⊆ C - (Top Secret, {NUC,...

section_summaries.heading: MAC Overview
section_summaries.summary: - Advantages: - Very secure - Centralized enforcement - Disadvantages: - May be too restrictive - Need additional mechanisms to implement multi-level security system - Security administration is difficult

section_summaries.heading: RBAC Motivation
section_summaries.summary: Sep 2009 Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - Multi-user systems - Multi-application systems - Permissions are associated with roles - Role-permission assignments are persistent v.s. user-permission assignments - Intuitive: competency, authority...

section_summaries.heading: Motivation
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - Express organizational policies - Separation of duties - Delegation of authority - Flexible: easy to modify to meet new security requirements - Supports - Least-privilege - Separation of duties - Data...

section_summaries.heading: Roles
section_summaries.summary: - User group: collection of user with possibly different permissions - Role: mediator between collection of users and collection of permissions - RBAC independent from DAC and MAC (they may coexist) - RBAC is policy neutral: configuration of RBAC determines the policy to be...

section_summaries.heading: RBAC
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology - RBAC3 consolidated model - RBAC1 - role hierarchy - RBAC2 - constraints - RBAC0 base model

section_summaries.heading: RBAC0
section_summaries.summary: - U - Users - R - Roles - P - Permissions - S - Sessions - User assignment - Permission assignment

section_summaries.heading: RBAC0
section_summaries.summary: - User: human beings - Role: job function (title) - Permission: approval of a mode of access - Always positive - Abstract representation - Can apply to single object or to many

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: RBAC0
section_summaries.summary: - UA: user assignments - Many-to-many - PA: Permission assignment - Many-to-many - Session: mapping of a user to possibly many roles - Multiple roles can be activated simultaneously - Permissions: union of permissions from all roles - Each session is associated with a single...

section_summaries.heading: RBAC0 Components
section_summaries.summary: Users, Roles, Permissions, Sessions - PA ⊆ P x R (many-to-many) - UA ⊆ U x R (many-to-many) - user: S 🡪 U, mapping each session si to a single user user(si) - roles: S 🡪 2R, mapping each session si to a set of roles: - roles(si) ⊆ {r | (user(si), r) ∈ UA} and si has permissions...

section_summaries.heading: RBAC0
section_summaries.summary: - Permissions apply to data and resource objects only - Permissions do NOT apply to RBAC components - Administrative permissions: modify U,R,S,P - Session: under the control of user to - Activate any subset of permitted roles - Change roles within a session

section_summaries.heading: RBAC1
section_summaries.summary: Structuring roles Inheritance of permission from junior role (bottom) to senior role (top) Partial order - Reflexive - Transitive - Anti-symmetric

section_summaries.heading: RBAC1
section_summaries.summary: Role Hierarchy

section_summaries.heading: RBAC1
section_summaries.summary: Role Hierarchy - Primary-care - Physician - Physician - Specialist - Physician - Health-care provider - Inheritance - of - privileges

section_summaries.heading: RBAC1 Components
section_summaries.summary: - Same as RBAC0: Users, Roles, Permissions, Sessions, PA ⊆ P x R, UA ⊆ U x R, user: S 🡪 U, mapping each session si to a single user user(si) - RH ⊆ R x R, partial order (≥ dominance) - roles: S 🡪 2R, mapping each session si to a set of roles - roles(si) ⊆ {r | (∃r’ ≥ r)...

section_summaries.heading: General Concepts
section_summaries.summary: - Users, Groups, Processes, Files - Each user has a unique UID - Each group has a unique GID - Each process has a unique PID - Users belong to multiple groups GID - Objects whose access is controlled - Files - Directories - Organization of Objects - Files are arranged in a...

section_summaries.heading: Basic Permissions Bits on Files
section_summaries.summary: - Permission: - Read: control reading the content of a file - Write: controls changing the content of a file - Execute: controls loading the file then execute - Many operations can be performed only by the owner of the file

section_summaries.heading: Where are Permission Bits Kept?
section_summaries.summary: - Each file/directory has associated an i-node. - The file type, permissions, owner UID and owner GID are save on disk in the inode of a file or directory

section_summaries.heading: Permission Bits on Directories
section_summaries.summary: - Read: for showing file names in a directory - Execution: for traversing a directory - does a lookup, allows one to find inode # from file name - ‘chdir’ to a directory requires execution - Write + execution: for creating/deleting files in the directory - requires no...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: The Three Sets of Permission Bits
section_summaries.summary: - Permission example - `drwxr-xr-x` - First: directory or not - Next three: owner permission - if the user is the owner of a file then the r/w/x bits for owner apply - Next three: group permission - if the user belongs to the group the file belongs to then the r/w/x bits for...

section_summaries.heading: Where are Permission Bits Kept?
section_summaries.summary: - Each file/directory has associated an inode. - The file type, permissions, owner UID and owner GID are save on disk in the inode of a file or directory

section_summaries.heading: Users vs. Subjects
section_summaries.summary: - Permission bits talk about what users can access a file - but it is subjects (processes) to perform actions on files - When a subject accesses a file, the system check which user it is acting on behalf of - Problem: what if an executable need stronger permission than the...

section_summaries.heading: Real User ID vs. Effective User ID
section_summaries.summary: - Each process has three user IDs - real user ID (ruid): owner of the process - effective user ID (euid): used in most access control decisions, often the same as ruid unless there is a change - saved user ID (suid): keeps the previous euid if it was a change - and three group...

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: The setuid flag
section_summaries.summary: - When used for a file - allows certain processes to have more than ordinary privileges while still being executable by ordinary users - When set, the effective uid of the calling process takes the value of the owner of the file

section_summaries.heading: Sep 2009
section_summaries.summary: Information Security by Van K Nguyen Hanoi University of Technology

section_summaries.heading: How the process user IDs work
section_summaries.summary: - When a process is created by `fork` - it inherits all three UIDs from its parent process - When a process executes a file by `exec` - if (the setuid bit of the file is off) - it keeps its three user IDs - otherwise // the setuid is set - euid of the process = ruid of the file...

section_summaries.heading: How to solve the passwd problem and the likes?
section_summaries.summary: - Passwd is owned by root and setuid is set - When a process executes it, then effective user becomes root, so the program runs as root on behalf of the user (only within the passwd work) - Can be a security flaw if the mechanism for temporary higher privilege is abused
